question_text: Which statement best explains why skydivers fall faster when they point their body downwards rather than in a spread-eagle position?

options.label: A
options.text: Their mass increases when they point downwards

options.label: B
options.text: Gravity pulls harder on a streamlined shape

options.label: C
options.text: A streamlined shape reduces air resistance, allowing a greater net downward force and faster acceleration

options.label: D
options.text: The air is thinner lower down, so there is less resistance

correct_answer: A streamlined shape reduces air resistance, allowing a greater net downward force and faster acceleration
explanation: When a skydiver points downwards, they reduce their cross-sectional area (the area facing the air). This smaller area means less air resistance pushes upwards against them. Since gravity pulls downwards with the same force, but air resistance is now smaller, the net downward force increases. A larger net force causes faster acceleration, so the skydiver moves faster. Spreading out like an eagle increases the cross-sectional area, which increases air resistance and reduces the net downward force.
